content.heading: Strings — text in a variable (instead of numbers)

blocks.alt: Visual explainer of strings in JavaScript — showing text, numbers, and symbols all wrapped in quotes
blocks.caption: Anything inside quotes is a string: letters, numbers, symbols, even emojis. The quotes are what makes it text.

blocks.text: A string is any text you put inside quotes. JavaScript doesn't care what's in there: letters, numbers, symbols, spaces, emojis. If it's in quotes, it's a string. Strings are one of the most common things you'll work with.

blocks.desc: Joining two or more strings together is called concatenation. The + sign does it.
Every string has a built-in length property. It tells you how many characters are inside.

blocks.label: Strings vs Numbers
blocks.text: '5' and 5 are completely different things. '5' is text. 5 is a number. '5' + '5' gives you '55'. 5 + 5 gives you 10. This will trip you up, so watch for it.
Think of Template Literals as a fill-in-the-blank sentence. `${VariableName}` marks the gaps where variables get dropped in. Wrap the whole sentence in backtick (`) quotes.

blocks.alt: A template literal using backticks with ${name} and ${age} dropped directly into the sentence

blocks.label: Easy to mix up
blocks.text: Backtick (`) is not the same as a single quote ('). If your template literal isn't working, that's the first thing to check.

lines: let intro = `My name is ${firstName} and I am ${age} years old.`;
console.log(intro);  // My name is Alex and I am 13 years old.

blocks.text: The `+` sign does different things depending on what's on each side of it. With two numbers it adds. With a string on either side, it joins the values together as text. JavaScript checks the types first, then decides which job to do.

blocks.alt: Two examples side by side: 5 + 5 with both values labeled as numbers giving 10, and '5' + 5 with the string highlighted differently giving '55'
blocks.caption: Same + sign, different result. The types on each side decide whether it adds or joins.

blocks.label: Remember!
blocks.text: If either value is a string, + treats the whole thing as text and joins. It only adds when both sides are numbers.

exercises.title: Spot the string
tasks: Which of these four values is a string?
exercises.hint: One thing makes a value a string — look for it in each option.
exercises.explanation: Quotes — single or double — are what turn a value into a string. 42 is a number, true is a boolean, and score is a variable name, not the value stored inside it.

exercises.title: What does + print here?
tasks: Read the code. What does it print to the console?
exercises.hint: Is there a string on either side of the +?

options.label: an error — you can't join strings and numbers

exercises.explanation: When a string is on either side of +, JavaScript joins everything as text. 'Your score: ' is a string, so + turns 100 into text and joins it. This is how you build messages in Simon Says.

exercises.title: String or Number?
tasks: Drag each value into the correct bucket.
exercises.hint: Quotes make a string. No quotes on a number means it's a real number. Watch out for traps.
exercises.explanation: The quotes are the only thing that matters. '100' and '0' look like numbers but they're strings — the quotes make them text. This is exactly why '5' + 5 gives '55' instead of 10.

exercises.title: Wrap it in backticks
tasks: Drop the right quotes to turn this into a working template literal.
exercises.hint: Template literals need a special quote — not single, not double. It's the key at the top-left of your keyboard.
exercises.explanation: Template literals must start and end with backticks (`). Single quotes and double quotes don't allow ${...} — they'd print it literally as '${playerName}' instead of the variable's value.

exercises.title: Add the missing space
tasks: Drop the right token so fullName has a space between first and last name.
exercises.hint: JavaScript won't add spaces on its own. You need to include a space character as its own piece.
exercises.explanation: ' ' is a string that contains a single space. Without it, you'd get 'JordanLee'. JavaScript joins exactly what you give it — no automatic spacing.

exercises.title: Put the greeting in order
tasks: Drag these lines into the correct order so the greeting prints correctly.
exercises.hint: You have to declare a variable before you can use it. Which line must come first?
codeLines: let message = greeting + ' Your score: ' + score;
exercises.explanation: Variables must be declared before they're used. greeting uses playerName, and message uses both greeting and score — so all three variables must be declared first.

exercises.title: Fill in the template
tasks: Type the template expressions to insert the player name and score.
exercises.hint: Inside a template literal, you drop variables in with ${ } — put the variable name inside the curly braces.
exercises.explanation: ${name} and ${score} are the correct expressions. The dollar sign and curly braces are both required — $name and $(score) won't work.

exercises.title: Trace the output
tasks: Follow the code step by step. What does the console print?
exercises.hint: JavaScript reads left to right. What happens the moment it hits a string?
exercises.explanation: JavaScript reads left to right. 'Total: ' + 10 joins as 'Total: 10' (a string). Then 'Total: 10' + 20 joins again as 'Total: 1020'. Once a string enters the chain, every + after it joins instead of adds. Fix: 'Total: ' + (a + b).

exercises.title: Measure the word
tasks: Type the property that gives you how many characters are in a string.
exercises.hint: Every string has a built-in property for this. You've seen it in the lesson.
exercises.explanation: Every string has a built-in .length property. 'Jordan' has 6 characters, so playerName.length is 6. You can use this in Simon Says to check if a player typed a response that's long enough.

exercises.title: Build a welcome message
tasks: Hit Run — see the blank message that prints.
Build message using + to join the pieces: 'Welcome, ' + playerName + '! Your score is ' + score + '.'
Hit Run. The console should print: Welcome, Sam! Your score is 0.
exercises.hint: Join each piece with + between them. Don't forget the space after the comma and the period at the end.

steps.text: Hit Run — see the blank message that prints.
Build message by joining the pieces with +. Hit Run.
Your message is live. Change playerName to your own name and run it again!

exercises.title: Fix the broken template literal
tasks: Hit Run and read the console. The output should surprise you.
Find what's wrong: ${name} and ${level} are printing literally instead of inserting the values.
Fix the quotes on line 3. Hit Run and confirm the correct output.
exercises.hint: Template literals need backticks, not single quotes. Check what kind of quotes surround the message on line 3.

steps.text: Hit Run and read the console. Notice ${name} and ${level} are printing literally.
Fix line 3: change the single quotes to backticks so the template literal works. Hit Run.
The backtick is the only quote that activates ${...}. Single and double quotes treat it as plain text.

checks.includes: My name is Alex and I am on level 5.
checks.label: correct message is printed

exercises.title: Build the Simon Says intro
tasks: Fill in the template literal to build: 'Ready, Jordan? Level 1 starts now. You have 3 lives.'
Hit Run to confirm. Then change playerName to your own name.
exercises.hint: Start inside the backticks: `Ready, ${playerName}? Level ${level} starts now. You have ${lives} lives.`

steps.text: Fill in the template literal between the backticks. Use ${playerName}, ${level}, and ${lives}. Hit Run.
Now change playerName to your own name on line 1. Hit Run and see the intro update automatically.
That's the power of template literals — one change to the variable, the whole message updates.

checks.label: intro is printed to the console

challenges.title: The broken calculator
challenges.description: A student built a score calculator. They stored the starting score as '200' because they copied it from a text box. When they add 50 for a correct answer, the score shows '20050' instead of 250. Explain why this happens and rewrite the code so the math works correctly.
challenges.hint: Text boxes always give you a string, even if the value looks like a number. Check how score is declared.
challenges.explanation: score is '200' in quotes, making it a string, not a number. So '200' + 50 joins as text and gives '20050'. Fix: remove the quotes (let score = 200) or convert it with Number(score) before adding.

challenges.title: The name badge machine
challenges.description: A school event needs name badges. Each badge should say "Hi, my name is [first] [last] and I am from [city]." You have three variables: firstName, lastName, city. But the machine is printing "Hi, my name is JordanLeeAtlanta", with no spaces or punctuation. Find what's wrong and fix it.
challenges.hint: Strings join exactly as written. JavaScript won't add spaces or punctuation for you.

checks.includes: Hi, my name is Jordan Lee and I am from Atlanta.
checks.label: prints the correct badge message

challenges.explanation: The + sign joins strings with nothing in between unless you add it yourself. Fix: add spaces and the missing phrase: 'Hi, my name is ' + firstName + ' ' + lastName + ' and I am from ' + city + '.'

challenges.title: The mystery output
challenges.description: Before running this code, predict both outputs. Then explain in one sentence why the two lines give different results even though they use the same three variables.
challenges.hint: JavaScript reads left to right. What happens the moment it hits a string?
challenges.explanation: JavaScript evaluates left to right. In a + b + c, the first + sees a string on the left so it joins: '10' + 20 = '1020', then '1020' + 30 = '102030'. In b + c + a, the first + sees two numbers so it adds: 20 + 30 = 50, then 50 + '10' = '5010'. Same variables, different order, different result.
